basics.name: Katie Caruso
basics.label: PhD Student — Microbiology & Bioengineering
basics.summary: I study applications of cold-adapted bacterial isolates for biocementation in permafrost environments at Montana State University's Center for Biofilm Engineering. My research spans microbiology, bioengineering, ecology, and remote sensing, with a foundation in open science and reproducible methods.

work.name: Center for Biofilm Engineering, Montana State University
work.position: Graduate Researcher — Biocementation in Permafrost Environments
work.summary: Studying applications of cold-adapted bacterial isolates for microbially-induced calcium carbonate precipitation (MICP) to stabilize materials in thawing permafrost environments.
highlights: Characterizing growth and urease activity of glacier-derived ureolytic bacterial isolates from Gilkey Glacier and Glacier National Park
Developing protocols for carbon source optimization in cold-adapted MICP systems
Planning fieldwork in Alaska (Summer 2026) to collect permafrost-specific isolates
NSF Extreme Biofilms National Research Traineeship recipient

work.name: NOAA NCEI, NASA DEVELOP National Program
work.position: North Carolina Center Lead & Communications Fellow
work.summary: Designed, managed, and supervised applied research projects using NASA Earth observations for environmental management decisions.
highlights: Designed and managed six cross-disciplinary research projects applying NASA Earth observations
Supervised six project teams of 3–5 researchers each
Built partnerships with 14 academic, government, and non-profit organizations
Supported Introductory and Advanced Python Software Carpentry Workshops

work.name: NASA Ames Research Center & UGA Center for Geospatial Research, NASA DEVELOP
work.summary: Applied remote sensing and geospatial analysis to ecological forecasting problems.
highlights: Modeled current and future Venus flytrap (Dionaea muscipula) habitat suitability using Landsat 8, PALSAR, Sentinel-2, and Terra MODIS
Monitored spongy moth (Lymantria dispar) defoliation in New England forests
Created Google Earth Engine GUI for federal partners to monitor forest defoliation

work.name: Conserving Carolina
work.summary: Developed environmental service-learning programming and mentored participants in conservation work.

work.name: Hemlock Restoration Initiative
work.summary: Protected hemlock trees through monitoring and chemical treatment of hemlock woolly adelgid.

work.name: Oʻahu National Wildlife Refuge Complex
work.position: Kupu Hawaiʻi AmeriCorps Member — Habitat Restoration
work.summary: Restored dunes, wetlands, and dry forests through native plantings and invasive species removal.
highlights: Coordinated over 400 outplantings in dry forest restoration plot
Managed nursery of over 40 native species and created digital record-keeping database

work.name: McCullough Institute, UNC Asheville
work.summary: Examined Spiraea virginiana sexual reproduction through fieldwork, labwork, and statistical analysis.

work.name: Southern Appalachian CESU, U.S. Park Service
work.summary: Investigated ectomycorrhizal fungi shifts associated with eastern hemlock decline from hemlock woolly adelgid infestation.
highlights: Published first-author peer-reviewed article in American Midland Naturalist (2021)

work.name: NEMAC
work.summary: Communicated disaster research through written and visual tools for Landslides website and Drought.gov.

courses: NSF Extreme Biofilms National Research Traineeship

education.area: Molecular and Cell Biology of Symbiosis

courses: Thesis: Assessing the effect of eastern hemlock decline from hemlock woolly adelgid infestation on ectomycorrhizal colonization and growth of red oak seedlings
